What FY26 really proved

TDPS spent years building quietly (OEM approvals, certifications, export relationships) and FY26 is when that infrastructure started paying out at scale. 

Revenue grew 44%, profit grew even faster, and the factory is so full that customers are questioning 3-day delays. That's not luck. That's compounding.

Management has guided ₹2,400-2,500+ crore for FY27, with FY28 pointing toward ₹3,000-3,200 crore - all on existing capacity. The large generator expansion targeting up to 200 MW hasn't even been announced yet. When it lands, it resets the ceiling.

The constraint isn't demand. It isn't capital. It's just execution - and management's language suggests they know it, and intend to deliver.

What didn't drive growth

Equally important is what didn't work - each of these tells you something useful about what FY27 and beyond might look like.

Operating leverage

Here's the part that made profit growth even better than revenue growth. TDPS runs a fixed-cost-heavy operation - plant, machinery, a skilled engineering team.

As revenue scaled to ₹590 crore per quarter, the same cost base was now spread across a much larger base. The hiring was largely done; incremental revenue was flowing through at higher margins than the reported average.

The result: PAT grew 42% while revenue grew 35%. The one active headwind is commodity inflation - copper is running near $14,000/MT and hedges are gradually running out. 

Management expects margins to hold over the next 2-3 quarters, but this is the variable to watch closely.

Going export-heavy

The second driver wasn't a segment - it was a change in who TDPS was selling to. Export orders grew at 76%, nearly 1.5× the pace of total inflow growth. By year-end, 4 out of every 5 rupees of new orders was export-linked.

Export contracts priced in USD and EUR mean that as the rupee depreciates, INR revenue rises - and since most raw materials are domestically sourced, that benefit flows largely to the bottom line. 

More importantly, export contracts tied to global OEM approvals are sticky in a way domestic orders simply aren't. Getting approved as a TDPS supplier takes years of qualification. Once you're in, the switching costs are too high to justify moving.

The engine: gas turbine generators

TDPS makes generators that sit alongside gas turbines and engines. Every turbine needs one generator. So when turbine manufacturers start producing more, TDPS's order book grows proportionally - they don't need to win new customers or fight for share. They just need their existing OEM relationships to stay intact and their customers' factories to run hot.

That's exactly what happened. Global demand for gas-based power infrastructure (driven by AI data centers needing reliable, dispatchable power) sent turbine OEMs into aggressive capacity expansion mode. Management described it simply as "massive growth, without pause."

One important nuance: all of FY26's gas turbine growth came from sub-50 MW machines. The large generator expansion (targeting up to 200 MW) that management is now planning is a completely separate, incremental story - one that hasn't contributed a single rupee yet. That's the next chapter.

Overview

FY26 was a breakout year. Consolidated revenue crossed ₹1,878 crore (+44%) and PAT grew even faster than the topline at +42%. Margins expanded.

But this wasn't broad-based, all-round growth. Three things did the work: gas turbine generators, a shift toward exports, and operating leverage. Everything else was either flat or a drag.

TD Power Systems Ltd : Understanding The Revenue Expansion

FY26 was a landmark year for TD Power Systems. Revenue grew 44%, profits scaled even faster, and order inflows hit record levels.

Here's a deep dive into the real drivers behind TDPS's revenue expansion and what it could mean for the years ahead. 🧵👇

📢 Company Update | Iris Clothings Limited

🔹 Iris Clothings has announced its entry into the Quick Commerce segment through one of India's dominant Quick Commerce players.

🔹 Products will initially be available across Bengaluru and Hyderabad, marking a strategic expansion of the Company's omnichannel distribution platform.

🔹 The initiative builds on the Company's existing distribution network comprising:
• Distributor-led sales
• Exclusive Brand Outlets (EBOs)
• Direct-to-Consumer (D2C) platform

🔹 The addition of Quick Commerce is expected to enhance customer accessibility, convenience, and brand visibility across multiple touchpoints.

🔹 The move is particularly relevant for the kidswear category, where purchases are often gifting-led, immediate, and occasion-driven.

🔹 Quick Commerce is rapidly emerging as a key retail channel in India, and the Company is well positioned to benefit from evolving consumer purchasing behaviour and unlock incremental growth opportunities.

India on Track for $1 Trillion Exports in FY27, Says Goyal; Trade Pact with US Only After India Secures Tariff Edge Over Rivals

The Big Target — On Track Despite Disruptions
India on track to meet $1 trillion exports target in FY27, despite disruptions caused by West Asia crisis, Commerce and Industry Minister Piyush Goyal said Saturday

Country has already achieved over 15% of the target

"I am confident that Indian exporters are well on the path to achieve the target I have set for FY27. With the FTAs (free trade agreements) and ease of doing business, I am certain we will achieve it" — Goyal to ET

Road to Record Exports — Key Numbers
15% of FY27's $1 trillion export target achieved despite Iran crisis

April-May exports up 14.7% YoY at $162.7 billion

India eyes $2 trillion exports annually over the next five years

The US Trade Pact — Conditional on Tariff Advantage
Goyal said India and US have finalised framework for proposed trade agreement, but deal will not take effect until India secures tariff advantage over competing exporting nations

"We cannot implement the FTA until we secure a competitive advantage. The issue currently pending is that our duties need to be lower compared to those of competing nations; once this is settled, the trade agreement will be implemented"

Context — Target Set Last Month
Goyal had said last month that India has set a target of achieving exports worth $1 trillion this year

Other Growth Signals — AI & Data Centres
AI startups to get major share of ₹10,000 crore Fund of Funds 2.0

Mumbai positioned as India's preferred data centre hub

Core Theme 
Goyal's confidence in India hitting its ambitious $1 trillion export target for FY27 — backed by a 14.7% YoY surge in April-May exports to $162.7 billion despite West Asia disruptions — reveals a calculated strategic patience on trade negotiations rather than urgency to close deals at any cost. By explicitly conditioning the "finalised" US trade pact's implementation on India first securing a tariff advantage over rival exporting nations, the government is signalling it would rather hold a framework agreement in reserve than risk locking in terms that could disadvantage Indian exporters relative to competitors — a stance that, combined with parallel pushes into AI startup funding and positioning Mumbai as a data centre hub, suggests India's $1 trillion export ambition is being pursued through diversified growth levers rather than dependence on any single bilateral deal materialising on Washington's timeline.

Sumeet Industries Limited's ₹199.75 Cr Rights Issue to Fund 140,000 TPA Capacity Expansion, Debt Reduction and Solar Project

Sumeet Industries Limited has launched a ₹199.75 Cr Rights Issue, to support its next phase of growth, led by the integration of the recently acquired Polyester Chips facility from Nakoda Limited.

A key objective of the Rights Issue is the operationalisation of the acquired 140,000 TPA Polyester Chips Plant from Nakoda Limited, Surat. The Company plans to invest ₹49 Cr from the issue proceeds towards this project, which is expected to be recommissioned in Q1 FY27-28 and will strengthen backward integration, enhance manufacturing scale, and support downstream polyester operations.

Key Terms of the Rights Issue:
* Rights Issue Size: ₹199.75 Cr
* Shares Offered: 16.84 Cr Equity Shares
* Issue Price: ₹11.86 per Share
* Face Value: ₹2 per Share
* Entitlement Ratio: 8 Rights Shares for every 25 Shares Held
* Record Date: 12 June 2026
* Issue Opens: 22 June 2026
* Last Date for Renunciation: 15 July 2026
* Issue Closes: 20 July 2026

Strategic Allocation of Net Funds (₹194.90 Cr):
* ₹49.90 Cr marked for Nakoda asset integration and operationalisation, supporting the Company's downstream POY & FDY operations.
* ₹100 Cr towards working capital to support higher production volumes and raw material procurement.
* ₹23 Cr towards debt repayment to reduce finance costs and strengthen the balance sheet.
* ₹22 Cr for a 6.5 MW captive solar power plant to lower power costs and enhance energy security.

The Rights Issue marks a significant milestone in Sumeet Industries' growth journey, with the Nakoda acquisition expected to support approximately 30% growth in Total Income in FY27 with EBITDA margins of 5.0%–6.0%. Operational Highlights
• Dry lease operations generated Total Income of ₹528.71 Cr during FY26
• Handled 24,353.42 tonnes of cargo volume across 1,923 trips during FY26

• Third aircraft operational
